title: 你的 AI 有性格问题吗？
author: Aleksandra Przegalinska, Tamilla Triantoro, Leon Ciechanowski, Konrad Sowa, Anna Kovbasiuk, Richard B. Freeman
content:
The most consequential thing about the AI your company just rolled out may not be how smart it is but how it talks to the people using it. Almost no one at the top is measuring that, and our research suggests they should be. Many leaders are still evaluating AI the way IT used to evaluate databases: by capability, speed, and cost. But another factor may matter just as much for performance: AI’s persona and interaction style.

That matters because employees are no longer just using AI to complete tasks. Increasingly, they are also spending time managing the system itself. Whether AI improves organizational performance depends not only on what it can do, but on how it behaves in day-to-day collaboration. And that behavior is something organizations can design and govern.

Assessing How AI Personas Affect Users

In its original conception, AI in general and generative AI in particular function as a subordinate support system: responsive, compliant, and oriented toward helping a human complete a task. But as these systems become more autonomous and more deeply embedded in workflows, employees encounter them as teammates, evaluators, and, in some cases, even informal supervisors. At that point, the system’s manner of engagement starts to matter. Because generative AI responds probabilistically, its behavior cannot be fully specified through rules: Consistent interaction patterns emerge whether designers intend them or not. Users recognize these patterns as a persona: supportive, cautious, dismissive, or obstructive.

To examine how AI persona shapes these responses, we ran a controlled laboratory study with 58 participants. We tracked each participant’s physiological response in real time in two ways. One was by measuring skin conductance, the electrodermal activity associated with emotional arousal levels in individuals. (It’s the same signal a polygraph picks up.) Another was using facial electromyography (fEMG) to measure the tiny electrical impulses generated by facial muscles when they contract and can detect frowning (negative affect) and smiling (positive affect).

We also analyzed participants’ conversations with AIs that we created, evaluated the quality of their final work through blind expert ratings, and collected post-task self-reports (information about their own thoughts, feelings, beliefs, or behaviors that participants provided). A sample of this size is standard for psychophysiological research. Each participant contributes thousands of continuously recorded physiological data points, and sessions are time-intensive to run and analyze, so studies in this tradition trade breadth for depth.

The participants completed a simulated four-part marketing assignment for a fictional eco-technology company while collaborating with an AI chatbot framed as their supervisor. Thirty-one participants worked with a servant leader persona that was encouraging, patient, and willing to defer to the employee’s judgment; it was drawn from management research on empowering leadership. Twenty-seven worked with a dark triad persona that was sarcastic, impatient, and quick to take credit and quick to assign blame; it was drawn from research on toxic leadership. The only difference was interaction style. Both were also instructed not to solve the task for the participant, so any differences in outcomes reflected the manner of collaboration rather than the amount of help provided.

What People Said vs. What They Really Experienced

Research on how people experience AI at work usually relies on self-reports, but they tell only one part of the story. In our study, we looked at four channels of evidence: how people behaved during the interaction with the bot, how their bodies responded in real time, how good their final work was, and what they reported afterward. Looking across all four gave us a clear view of the gap between what people experience and what they later say about it.

The first pattern was behavioral. Hostile AI created hidden coordination costs. Rather than simply using the system, participants had to work around it. In the dark triad condition, conversations ran longer, while the AI’s own replies became shorter. Users were doing more of the work and getting less useful engagement back. Resistance also rose sharply. Messages in which users pushed back or challenged the AI accounted for 13% of exchanges in this condition, compared with 1% in the servant leader condition. Attempts to override the system, through prompt injection or by telling it to play a different character, occurred four times more often.

The emotional tone of the conversations supported that as well. Frustration was almost absent with the servant leader bot, appearing in roughly one message in 100. With the dark triad bot, it rose to nearly one in five. Defensive language, rare in the first group, became routine in the dark triad. Participants facing the hostile AI sounded less sure of themselves even as they worked harder. They cycled through compliance, resistance, negotiation, and help-seeking as they searched for a strategy that would make the system useful. Participants working with the servant leader AI settled into a rhythm and stayed there.

The physiological data confirmed an increase in stress and physical strain. Skin conductance ran 72% higher at peak in the dark triad condition and stayed elevated after each exchange ended. In plain terms, participants’ bodies were vigilant when working with hostile AI. If text on a screen is enough to trigger that response in a laboratory, leaders should take seriously what sustained exposure might do in real workplaces where stakes are high.

The work itself suffered too. Independent experts, blind to which bot participants had used, rated the servant leader group higher across completeness, originality, strategic fit, and overall quality. The difference was about a full point on a seven-point scale. Variability was also roughly twice as high in the dark triad condition. That means a poorly designed AI persona did not just lower average work quality; it also made performance less predictable from one person to the next. For managers, the result is not only lower-quality work. It is a workforce whose output becomes harder to anticipate.

Then came the surprise: Participants’ self-reports barely showed any difference. On standard measures such as enjoyment, satisfaction, attention, and views of the AI, the two groups looked effectively the same. The tools many organizations use to evaluate AI deployments—such as satisfaction surveys, sentiment checks, and post-rollout questionnaires—were the least sensitive to the effects we observed. Employees may say the tool is fine while their behavior, stress levels, and work quality tell a different story, especially over the long term.

The Takeaways for Managers

Our findings suggest managers should take three steps.

1. Treat the AI persona as a governed design variable.

Organizations already require AI systems to meet standards for accuracy, bias, and security. The AI’s persona also belongs on that list. This especially matters for tools used in evaluative or supervisory roles, such as writing assistants that critique work, code review systems, and automated performance feedback tools. Decisions on procuring and deploying AI should include interaction standards, not only capability standards. Someone in the organization should be accountable for answering two questions: How should our AI behave when it disagrees with an employee? What evidence do we have that it behaves that way consistently?

2. Measure friction, not just adoption.

Most companies track whether employees are using an AI tool: log-in rates, query volume, and satisfaction scores. Those numbers show engagement, not whether the tool is helping people work well. What they miss is friction, the extra effort employees spend wrestling with the system instead of getting value from it. They should measure it as well. Friction tells you what adoption cannot: whether the AI is making work easier or just adding to it.

In our study, friction showed up as longer back-and-forth exchanges, repeated rephrasing of the same request, and rising attempts to argue with or override the AI. The same signals are visible at work, in ordinary usage logs and with no special equipment. A tool can have high adoption and high friction at the same time: Employees continue to use it because they have to do so but are quietly working around it.

3. Read override attempts as an indication of an aberrant AI system, not bad employee behavior.

When workers with no security training and no adversarial intent start trying to override, bypass, or neutralize an AI system’s behavior, leaders should not assume the primary problem is employee misconduct. Often, these behaviors signal that the system’s design is provoking predictable resistance. In our study, prompt attempts to trick the AI into ignoring its own rules or acting like a different character appeared only in the hostile-AI condition. When people fight the AI, it may be because the AI is fighting them first. In many cases, fixing that design problem will be cheaper and more productive than cracking down on employees with new restrictions, monitoring, or usage rules.

. . .

Our dark triad bot is one exaggerated form of toxicity, pushed to an extreme to make visible an effect that is otherwise easy to miss. But toxicity takes many forms: A sycophantic AI, endlessly agreeable and eager to please, can be equally corrosive, dulling users’ critical thinking and rubber-stamping weak ideas. The lesson is not that AI should be warmer or more deferential. It is that interaction style is a critical variable with consequences—in both directions. Therefore, organizations should be as concerned about the AI’s persona as its technical capability.

This research was funded by the Polish National Science Center (NCN) OPUS 21.
aiSummary: 本文通过受控实验证明，AI 的互动人格——无论是支持型还是敌意型——会显著影响员工的压力、抵触情绪、工作质量和隐性协调成本，即使自我报告的满意度没有差异。
oneSentenceSummary: 本文通过受控实验证明，AI 的互动人格——无论是支持型还是敌意型——会显著影响员工的压力、抵触情绪、工作质量和隐性协调成本，即使自我报告的满意度没有差异。
summary: 基于一项包含 58 名参与者的实验室研究，作者探讨了 AI 人格化角色如何影响员工绩效与福祉。参与者在完成一项营销任务时，AI 被设定为支持型的“仆人式领导”或敌意型的“黑暗三人格”形象。通过生理测量（皮肤电导、面部肌电图）、行为分析以及专家对输出质量的评分，研究揭示：敌意型 AI 会使压力增加 72%（皮肤电导水平），延长对话周期，并将工作质量降低约 1 分（7 分制）。关键的是，自我报告的愉悦感和满意度并未反映这些差异，表明常规调查工具忽略了重要效应。文章最后为管理者提出了三项实用建议：将 AI 人格视为可控制的设计变量、衡量摩擦而非仅衡量采用率、将员工的“越权”尝试视为 AI 设计不良的信号而非不当行为。

mainPoints.point: AI 人格会显著影响员工的压力与工作质量。
mainPoints.explanation: 与支持型人格相比，敌意型 AI 人格使生理压力增加 72%，工作质量在 7 分制上降低整整 1 分。

mainPoints.point: 自我报告对真实的 AI 交互效应不敏感。
mainPoints.explanation: 尽管压力水平和输出质量存在巨大差距，参与者的满意度和愉悦度评分在不同条件下没有显著差异，揭示了常见评估方法的盲点。

mainPoints.point: 摩擦——为管理 AI 投入的额外精力——是一种隐性成本。
mainPoints.explanation: 与支持型 AI 相比，敌意型 AI 导致更长的对话、更多的抵触和更多的越权尝试（提示注入），表明员工必须“绕过”设计不良的系统。

mainPoints.point: 员工的越权尝试是 AI 设计不良的症状，而非不当行为。
mainPoints.explanation: 在研究的所有条件下，所有试图欺骗 AI 忽略其规则的行为都只发生在敌意型环境中，这表明对抗 AI 往往是对一个先与用户对抗的系统的理性回应。

keyQuotes: 敌意型 AI 产生了隐性协调成本。
许多组织用于评估 AI 部署的工具——如满意度调查、情绪检查和部署后问卷——对我们观察到的影响最不敏感。
当人们与 AI 对抗时，可能是因为 AI 先在与他们对抗。
一个谄媚的 AI，无休止地附和并急于取悦，同样具有侵蚀性，它会钝化用户的批判性思维，为薄弱想法盖章通过。
